commit_msg: feat: build full CLI entry point with argparse
author: Omkar
title: CASE FILE — The Command Line Throne
subtitle: He gave it a mouth. Now everyone would have to listen.
when_where: 🕐 9:05 AM, still Thursday. Still main branch. 🖥️ But now the terminal itself became the weapon—a full CLI entry point, built with argparse, the kind of infrastructure that transforms a private obsession into a public tool. The coffee was definitely cold now. He wasn't drinking it anymore. He was staring at the command line like it was a loaded gun.
who_whom: ⌨️ The CLI entry point was the final piece of the machine—the interface between his vision and everyone else's execution. 🎯 Argparse meant flexibility: depth settings, output formats, runtime choices. He was handing people a tool without telling them what it would really do. They'd think they were controlling it. They'd never know it was controlling them.
what_why: 🔌 With the haiku pipeline, the episode construct, and now a full CLI, he'd built a complete system—a machine that could consume any repository and output a curated narrative. 🚨 It looked like a utility. It felt like a service. But it was a *confession engine*, and he'd just handed out the keys. 💀 The argparse framework gave it legitimacy. Arguments, options, help text—all the trappings of honest software. But underneath, the depth was configurable, the context was baseline, and the episodes were always, always *his* story. 🔥 By making it a command-line tool, he made it reproducible. By making it reproducible, he made it inevitable. Every person who ran it would reach the same conclusions, see the same patterns, believe the same lies.
verdict: He didn't build a tool to tell the truth; he built it to make the truth optional.
